title: The Complete Guide to .gitignore
excerpt: A comprehensive beginner-to-advanced guide to Git’s .gitignore file. Learn how .gitignore works, how to create and configure it, understand pattern syntax, avoid common mistakes, and keep sensitive files like API keys and environment variables out of your repository.
content:
What Is .gitignore?

If you use Git to manage your code, you have probably heard of the .gitignore file. But what exactly is it, and why does every developer need it?

A .gitignore file is a plain text file that tells Git which files and folders to ignore. When you run git add or git commit, Git looks at this file and skips everything listed in it. Those files will not be tracked, staged, or committed.

In simple terms: .gitignore keeps your Git repository clean by excluding files that do not belong there.

Why Does This Matter?

Without .gitignore, you might accidentally commit passwords, API keys, huge build folders, or editor config files.

These can cause security risks, slow down your repository, and clutter your project history.

Every professional project — big or small — uses a .gitignore file.

How Does .gitignore Work?

When Git checks your project folder for changes, it reads the .gitignore file. Any file or folder that matches a pattern in .gitignore is completely skipped by Git. It is as if those files do not exist from Git's perspective.


This check happens every time you run git commands. Git reads the .gitignore rules from top to bottom. Later rules can override earlier ones.

Where Do You Put the .gitignore File?

You can place .gitignore files in several locations. Each one affects a different scope.



  • Root .gitignore — This is the most common. Put it in your project's main folder. It covers the whole project.

  • Subfolder .gitignore — Rules only apply to that folder and its contents.

  • Global .gitignore — Applies to every Git project on your computer. Good for editor files like .DS_Store or Thumbs.db.

  • .git/info/exclude — Local rules that are never shared with others. Only you see them.


How to Create a .gitignore File

Creating a .gitignore file is very simple. Here are several ways to do it:

Method 1 - Create It Manually

Open your terminal and run this command in your project folder:

touch .gitignore

Then open the file in any text editor and add your rules.

Method 2 — Use GitHub's Template When Creating a Repo

When you create a new repository on GitHub, it offers a dropdown to pick a .gitignore template based on your language (Node, Python, Java, etc.). This is the fastest way to get started.


Method 3 — Use gitignore.io (Now toptal.com/developers/gitignore)


This is a free website where you type your tools (like "Node", "Python", "macOS", "VSCode") and it generates a ready-to-use .gitignore file. Just copy and paste.


Method 4 — Use Git Itself

# Set a global gitignore file 
git config --global core.excludesfile ~/.gitignore_global


.gitignore Syntax and Patterns

The rules inside a .gitignore file are called patterns. They are very flexible. Here is a full breakdown of how patterns work.


Pattern

What It Does

Example

# comment

Lines starting with # are comments

# This is a note


filename.ext

Ignores a specific file anywhere

secret.txt


foldername/

Ignores an entire folder

node_modules/


*.ext

Ignores all files with this extension

*.log


!filename

Un-ignores a previously ignored file

!important.log


/filename

Ignores only in the root folder

/config.env


folder/**

Ignores everything inside folder

build/**


**/*.ext

Ignores files in any subfolder

**/*.DS_Store


file?.txt

? matches exactly one character

file1.txt, filea.txt


[abc].txt

Matches one of the characters in [ ]

a.txt, b.txt, c.txt


Understanding Wildcards

Wildcards are special characters that match multiple files at once.

Wildcard Quick Reference

* (asterisk) — matches anything EXCEPT a slash. Use for file names.

** (double asterisk) — matches anything INCLUDING slashes. Use for deep paths.

? (question mark) — matches exactly ONE character.

[ ] (brackets) — matches any ONE character listed inside.

Negation Rules — The ! Trick

You can ignore a folder but keep specific files inside it using the ! prefix (negation).

# Ignore all log files 
*.log 
# But keep this important one 
!important.log 
# Ignore the build folder 
build/ 
# But keep one specific file inside it 
!build/README.mdcode

Important: Negation Limitation

You CANNOT un-ignore a file that is inside an ignored directory.

If you ignore build/ then !build/file.txt will NOT work.

To fix this, ignore specific files inside the folder instead of the whole folder.

Order of Rules

Rules are read from top to bottom. Later rules win over earlier ones when there is a conflict. Also, rules in a subfolder's .gitignore override the root .gitignore for that subfolder.

Full .gitignore Examples by Language / Framework

Node.js / JavaScript

# Dependencies
node_modules/

# Build output
dist/
build/

# Logs
*.log
npm-debug.log*
yarn-error.log*

# Environment variables (NEVER commit these)
.env
.env.local
.env.*.local

# OS files
.DS_Store
Thumbs.db

# Editor
.vscode/
.idea/
*.swp

Python

# Byte-compiled files
__pycache__/
*.py[cod]
*.pyo

# Virtual environments
venv/
env/
.env/

# Distribution / packaging
dist/
build/
*.egg-info/

# Testing
.pytest_cache/
.coverage
htmlcov/

# Jupyter Notebook checkpoints
.ipynb_checkpoints/

# Environment variables
.env
*.env

Java / Maven / Gradle

# Compiled class files
*.class

# Package files
*.jar
*.war
*.ear

# Build directories
target/
build/
.gradle/

# IDE files
.idea/
*.iml
.eclipse/
.settings/

# Logs
*.log

React / Next.js

# Dependencies
node_modules/

# Next.js build output
.next/
out/

# Production
build/

# Environment files
.env*
!.env.example

# Debug
npm-debug.log*
yarn-debug.log*

# Vercel
.vercel

General / Universal Rules

# macOS
.DS_Store
.AppleDouble
.LSOverride

# Windows
Thumbs.db
ehthumbs.db
Desktop.ini

# Linux
*~

# Editor backups
*.bak
*.tmp
*.swp

# IDE
.vscode/
.idea/
*.sublime-workspace

Important Concepts You Must Know


Already Tracked Files Are NOT Ignored

This is the most common mistake beginners make. If you already committed a file to Git, adding it to .gitignore will NOT make Git forget it. Git is still tracking it.


How to Fix It — Remove a Tracked File from Git

Step 1: Add the file to .gitignore

Step 2: Run this command to stop tracking it:

git rm --cached filename.txt

(For a folder: git rm -r --cached foldername/)

Step 3: Commit the change:

git add .gitignore

git commit -m "Stop tracking filename.txt"

Now Git will ignore it going forward. The file stays on your computer.


The .gitignore File Itself Should Be Committed

Yes! The .gitignore file is meant to be committed and shared with your team. Everyone on the project should use the same ignore rules.


Commit This

git add .gitignore

git commit -m "Add gitignore"

git push

NEVER Commit This

git add .env

# NEVER commit .env

# Use .gitignore to block it


Checking If a File Is Ignored

You can check if a specific file is being ignored by Git with this command:

git check-ignore -v filename.txt

Git will tell you which rule in which .gitignore file is causing the file to be ignored. Very useful for debugging.

Seeing All Ignored Files

To see a list of all currently ignored files in your project, run:

git status --ignored

Setting Up a Global .gitignore

A global .gitignore applies to all Git projects on your computer. This is great for editor files and OS files that you never want to commit — no matter which project you are in.

Step 1 - Create the Global File

touch ~/.gitignore_global

Step 2 - Tell Git About It

git config --global core.excludesfile ~/.gitignore_global

Step 3 - Add Your Rules

# macOS
.DS_Store

# Windows
Thumbs.db

# VSCode
.vscode/

# JetBrains IDEs
.idea/

# Vim
*.swp
*.swo

Common .gitignore Mistakes and How to Fix Them

MistakeFix

Ignoring a file that was already committed

Use git rm --cached to untrack it first


Trying to un-ignore files inside an ignored folder

Ignore specific files, not the whole folder


Using wrong path separators (backslash on Windows)

Always use forward slashes / in .gitignore


Forgetting to commit the .gitignore file itself

Run git add .gitignore and commit it


Ignoring .env but forgetting .env.local or .env.production

Use .env* to catch all variants


Copying someone else's .gitignore without reviewing it

Always read and customize for your project



Useful Git Commands Related to .gitignore

# Check if a file is ignored and why
git check-ignore -v path/to/file

# List all ignored files
git status --ignored

# Stop tracking a single file
git rm --cached filename.txt

# Stop tracking a whole folder
git rm -r --cached foldername/

# Force-add an ignored file (use carefully)
git add -f filename.txt

# See all git config settings including global gitignore
git config --list

# Set global gitignore path
git config --global core.excludesfile ~/.gitignore_global


Security - What to ALWAYS Ignore

One of the most important uses of .gitignore is keeping sensitive data out of your repository. Once a secret is pushed to GitHub (even to a private repo), it should be considered compromised.


Files You Should ALWAYS Ignore

.env (environment variables — API keys, passwords, database URLs)

*.pem, *.key, *.cert (SSL/TLS certificates and private keys)

id_rsa, id_ed25519 (SSH private keys)

config/secrets.yml (Rails and other framework secret files)

*.pfx (Windows certificate stores)

credentials.json (Google service account keys)

serviceAccountKey.json (Firebase admin keys)

*.keystore (Android signing keys)

If you ever accidentally commit a secret, change it immediately — rotate the key or password. Then use git filter-branch or BFG Repo Cleaner to remove it from history.

Advanced .gitignore Topics

How Multiple .gitignore Files Interact

When you have .gitignore files in multiple folders, they all work together. Git reads all of them and combines the rules. The file closest to your actual file has the highest priority.

Using .gitignore with Monorepos

A monorepo is a single Git repository that holds multiple projects. You can have one root .gitignore plus individual .gitignore files per sub-project.


# Root .gitignore (applies to all sub-projects)
.DS_Store
Thumbs.db
*.log

# Sub-project specific
# packages/frontend/.gitignore
node_modules/
dist/

# packages/backend/.gitignore
__pycache__/
*.pyc
.env

Template Repositories and .gitignore

If you create a template repository on GitHub, always include a well-crafted .gitignore. This saves every person who uses your template from having to set it up themselves.

Quick Reference Cheat Sheet

# ── IGNORE PATTERNS CHEAT SHEET ─────────────────────

filename.txt          # Exact file name
*.txt                 # All .txt files
docs/*.txt            # .txt files only in docs/ folder
docs/**/*.txt         # .txt files in docs/ and all subfolders
build/                # Entire folder
/config.js            # Only config.js in root folder
temp-?                # temp-1, temp-a, temp-b, etc.
[Tt]emp               # Temp or temp

# ── NEGATION ─────────────────────────────────────────
*.log                 # Ignore all .log files
!important.log        # But keep this one

# ── COMMENTS ────────────────────────────────────────
# This line is a comment and is ignored by Git

# ── BLANK LINES ─────────────────────────────────────
                      # Blank lines are also ignored
